title: const-correct pointer interfaces

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for const-correct pointer interfaces; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to const-correct pointer interfaces
sources.claim: C const qualification can protect the pointed-to object through one access path, the pointer object itself, or both, depending on declarator placement.

sources.locator: SEI CERT C Coding Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to const-correct pointer interfaces
sources.claim: C const qualification can protect the pointed-to object through one access path, the pointer object itself, or both, depending on declarator placement.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C const qualification can protect the pointed-to object through one access path, the pointer object itself, or both, depending on declarator placement.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: const int *p permits p to select another int but prevents modifying an int through p. Your goal is to design a pointer-based function signature with the least required mutation rights. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: One-dimensional arrays; Parameters and return values; Addresses and dereference; Declarations, prototypes and definitions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C const qualification can protect the pointed-to object through one access path, the pointer object itself, or both, depending on declarator placement. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: const does not make an originally mutable object globally immutable, casts can discard qualifiers unsafely, and nested pointer qualification has stricter compatibility rules. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: const int * and int * const mean the same thing because both contain the word const.

includes: Distinguish pointer constness from pointee constness and express read-only contracts.
Design a pointer-based function signature with the least required mutation rights.
excludes: const does not make an originally mutable object globally immutable, casts can discard qualifiers unsafely, and nested pointer qualification has stricter compatibility rules.

outcomes: Design a pointer-based function signature with the least required mutation rights.

examples.title: const-correct pointer interfaces: defined case
examples.situation: const int *p permits p to select another int but prevents modifying an int through p.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: const int *p permits p to select another int but prevents modifying an int through p.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Read the declarator from the identifier: const int * protects the pointee through that path, while int * const makes the pointer itself nonassignable.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Read the declarator from the identifier: const int * protects the pointee through that path, while int * const makes the pointer itself nonassignable.

examples.title: const-correct pointer interfaces: boundary case
examples.situation: int * const p keeps p fixed on one int while permitting modification of that int through p.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: int * const p keeps p fixed on one int while permitting modification of that int through p.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: const does not make an originally mutable object globally immutable, casts can discard qualifiers unsafely, and nested pointer qualification has stricter compatibility rules.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: const int * and int * const mean the same thing because both contain the word const.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design a pointer-based function signature with the least required mutation rights.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: const int * and int * const mean the same thing because both contain the word const.
misconceptions.correction: Read the declarator from the identifier: const int * protects the pointee through that path, while int * const makes the pointer itself nonassignable.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: const int * and int * const mean the same thing because both contain the word const.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model const-correct pointer interfaces: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
const does not make an originally mutable object globally immutable, casts can discard qualifiers unsafely, and nested pointer qualification has stricter compatibility rules.
